data.title: Topicus: Recurring VMS Revenues And M&A Discipline Remain Bullish
data.description:
Topicus.com Inc. (TOITF) is a part of the Constellation Software Inc. (CSI) ecosystem. TOITF is a vertical market software (VMS) holding company, with independently managed business units serving end markets that include education, healthcare, government, finance, and legal, among others. The company combines two large families: the Total Specific Solutions (TSS) and the Netherlands-based Topicus platform cluster. Last year, Topicus’s collaborations in healthcare with Better improved by using the same data layer, as well as TSS’s acquisitions of Cipal Schaubroeck and a part of Asseco Poland. And overall, I believe TOITF’s recurring revenue base still justifies a long-term bullish outlook at these levels.

Rolling Up VMS Businesses

Topicus is technically a holding company of vertical market software (VMS) firms across Europe. TOITF has independently managed software business units, operating across several countries, such as the Netherlands, France, Germany, the Nordics, and the United Kingdom, that serve verticals like education, healthcare, government, finance, legal, real estate, and retail. However, TOITF itself is a spin-out from Constellation Software Inc. (CSI), which was created in 2021.

Nevertheless, TOITF remains part of CSI’s ecosystem, and CSI retains an economic participation in TOITF (around 30%). Additionally, CSI also exerts controls on TOITF through its voting rights. I previously covered TOITF in April last year, and in the following months, the stock appreciated nicely. Unfortunately, around July 2025, the stock eventually topped out, and it’s now down approximately 43% from its 2025 highs, which is why I believe it’s worth revisiting this name.

You see, TOITF brings together CSI’s European VMS, which operates as Total Specific Solutions (TSS), with the Netherlands-based Topicus. Topicus clusters platform to develop software designed around the end user experience. This way, they engage clients within a shared digital environment that connects organizations, professionals, and their consumers. In essence, they form a decentralized SaaS ecosystem that targets a wide range of institutional clients.

For example, recently Topicus announced a collaboration in healthcare with Better, a provider of a digital health data platform. Topicus will use this platform to build multiple applications that plug into the same open data layer to make it easier to share patient data across hospitals, primary care, and community care organizations.

Acquisition First

Having said that, Topicus’s TSS is probably one of the company’s most important aspects. This portion of their business buys and builds their vertically integrated systems, which now comprises over 180 independent software companies. TOITF breaks this business down into two sub-groups with 1) TSS Public and 2) TSS Blue. TSS Public focuses on the public sector and healthcare in the Netherlands and the southern parts of Central Europe. On the other hand, TSS Blue specializes in private markets, with a geographic emphasis on the Netherlands, Germany, and the Nordics.

Fortunately, for TOITF, 2025 was again a notable year in terms of M&A. TSS finalized several new deals, ending the year with the acquisition of 23 companies that remained independent and kept their local identity. TSS invested a large amount of capital in two important transactions with Asseco Poland and Cipal Schaubroeck. Normally, TOITF generally buys out companies entirely, but in the Asseco Poland deal, TOITF only acquired a minority position of 24.84%. What’s interesting about Asseco is that it consists of a federation of several vertical market software businesses with local autonomy across more than 60 countries. So, in a way, TOITF bought a stake in a smaller version of itself. Plus, this was TSS's largest investment to date, which shows they’re doubling down on this growth through an M&A approach.

Additionally, TOITF finalized another large transaction in 2025, which was the acquisition of Cipal Schaubroeck. This is a Belgian local government software provider that offers suites for HR, payroll, security, hosting, and data management. Its customer base is centered on Flemish public institutions like municipalities, provinces, police zones, and social welfare. This acquisition complements TSS’s local government software, and it was a meaningful platform-scale in gov-tech that required a payment totaling $258 million.

Valuation And Upside Potential

Now, from a valuation perspective, TOITF currently trades at a $7.0 billion market cap. Their latest financial report (as of September 2025) shows their balance sheet holds $324.6 million in cash and short-term investments, against $873.1 million in financial debt (aside from other regular operating liabilities). Also, TOITF's latest weighted average shares outstanding is around 83.1 million. And their book value stood at $820.0 million, which would indicate a somewhat inflated P/B of 8.5. For comparison, their sector’s median P/B is much lower at around 3.9.

Moreover, using those figures, I estimate TOITF’s EV at around $7.6 billon. And, according to Seeking Alpha’s dashboard on TOITF, the company is projected to generate approximately $2.1 billion in revenues in 2026. So, that would suggest TOITF’s EV/S is roughly 3.65, which is actually slightly lower than its peers’ median forward EV/S of 3.7. So, for the most part, I believe TOITF is probably already trading around its fair value at these levels.

However, I do like that 2026 would imply that the company itself is growing at an above-average clip. Concretely, TOITF's forward topline growth is around 20.8%, which is much higher than the rest of its sector peers' growth of just 8.2%. So, based on that alone, I would actually argue that the stock deserves at the very least a premium above the sector median valuation multiples. Personally, I think a forward EV/S of 4.0 would be only 8.1% higher than its sector multiples, but would result in an implied EV of around $8.4 billion. So, using that figure, I estimate a tentative "fair" market cap of $7.85 billion, and my PPS price target of around $94.5.

Risk Analysis

Having said that, TOITF's cash flows seem to have significant fluctuations quarter over quarter. For instance, its latest Q3 figures suggest they generated only $55.0 million in positive cash flows. Note that I got that estimate by simply adding its quarterly cash flows from operations and CAPEX. Yet, using the same approach and TOITF’s TTM figures (as of September 2025), their positive cash flow increases to around $440.4 million (or $110.1 million per quarter on average). And, in that sense, TOITF would trade at a TTM P/CF multiple of only 15.2.

This is probably another good indicator that they’re now again in a compelling buy zone, as their peers’ P/CF is much higher at 19.1. However, volatile cash flows do make the stock itself riskier as well. And remember, my thesis depends on them executing prudently on their M&A strategy. If TOITF overpays for its acquisitions, while also failing to integrate its newly added subsidiaries, then it would be a double whammy for investors.

So, M&A is easily their main risk, because it often requires successful integration to fully realize its synergies. TOITF somewhat mitigates this risk because it mostly lets its subsidiaries continue business as usual post-acquisition. Nonetheless, even though that isn’t a big factor given TOITF’s business model, sizable write-downs due to a large failed M&A transaction could also send the stock much lower as a result.

Finally, using my figures, it's worth highlighting that TOITF's debt-to-cash ratio of 2.7 is somewhat high already. So, if their cash flow turns into cash burn for a few successive quarters, then it could constrain TOITF significantly. Fortunately, I believe they're a cash flow machine at the moment, which should give them ample ammunition to continue with their growth through M&A strategy. After all, TOITF essentially grows mainly through acquiring other firms. Plus, even though they're decentralized across Europe, if there's a sweeping EU regulation against their business model, you could see a quick pullback in the shares as a reaction.

But ultimately, given their positive cash flows, this growth strategy seems viable for now. Just note that they have less organic growth otherwise, which could backfire if their liquidity and cash flows ever dry up. In fact, only about 3% of its growth was organic, out of the 24% reported YoY growth this latest quarter. Thus, the best way to think of TOITF’s value proposition is through recurring maintenance revenue and client retention, which is then reinvested in further M&A to increase its topline.

Conclusion: Still A Compelling “Buy”

Overall, TOITF’s M&A strategy seems to continue to pay off. So far, they’ve accumulated a sizeable amount of recurring revenues through their acquisitions, mainly through maintenance services. I believe this shows they’re not buying into hype or dreams, but rather judiciously adding more shareholder value over time. In fact, I calculate that this type of recurring sales accounted for around 71.6% of its latest quarterly revenues. In my view, maintenance tends to lead to quite sticky revenues over time, because clients always need to keep their software in working condition regardless of macro cycles. So, given their relatively reasonable valuation multiples, I still think TOITF remains well-positioned to continue accumulating shareholder value over time for long-term investors.

data.title: Lear: A Solid Choice In Automotive Parts
data.description:
I recently updated my coverage on Adient (ADNT), where I gave Lear (LEA) as an alternative to the stock, and called Lear a solid/better choice than Adient for a multitude of reasons. Today, I will be updating on why this is such a solid choice, why I own the stock, and why I believe the company could be a market-beating longer-term investment.

This is not my first coverage of Lear. I have in fact covered the company for years - my last article can be found here, and is about 7 months old at this time. Since my last article, the TSR for the company has been more than twice that of SPY, and more importantly, significantly better than Adient.

You'll also note that it's not my first article on Lear where I am positive. I have been building a position in this company for over a year at a compelling price ($85-$95, roughly). The fact that the company now trades at about $119/share doesn't mean that it's unattractive though, it just means that we have some correction of the valuation, which, since about mid-2024 was troughing. Lear is, in my view, a perfect example of how to go about value-oriented investing, because the cycles for the company tend to be somewhat longer, and it tends to lack some of the immediate volatility of other stocks in the same sector.

In this article i'll revisit my targets for Lear, my expectations for the 2026-2028E period, and show you why, if you want to go into automotive supplies, Lear should be one of your first choices.

The yield and the credit rating are just part of the reason for why the company is attractive. It's not a bad idea to invest in one of the more vertically integrated suppliers in the entire automotive supply value chain - even if the valuation now is, by comparison, much higher than it once was.

Let's see what we have here.

Upside from automotive supply and parts

Like Adient, the company's primary operation is, at least in part, related to seating. Lear's revenues are primarily related to this sub-segment, which is what makes the company directly comparable to Adient - it manufactures everything from the basic structure to mechanisms to foams as well as headrests. It also installs things like heating and cooling in seating, but also focuses on additional features such as safety and intelligent functions. Lear also has its own leather and fabric for specialized covering and materials. Operations are international, but NA is a focal point for the company.

Secondary operational foci include electrical systems - specifically for new vehicle segments, such as BEV. Here, the company manufactures wiring harnesses, connectors, electronics, electrification (including battery management and chargers) as well as connected services.

The fact that the company has such a major part of its operations in seating and the inherent complexity of the seating manufacturing and operations, means that competitors wanting to enter this segment are few and far and few between. The work necessary to build up this sort of operation, not to mention create the confidence with suppliers and customers, can be characterized in well over a decade, with billions and billions of dollars. While some automotive manufacturers are moving to their own seating manufacturer, many still take advantage of what a company like Lear does.

The reasons why Lear is so interesting would include the degree of vertical integration that the company has. What I mean by this is that aside from a few specialized components and parts, the company manufactures most things on an in-house basis, which usually means efficiency, margins and lower costs for consumers.

Recently, Lear has also been investing more heavily in things like data integration, AI, and software automation to be able to cut these costs and enhance these upsides even further. A new term in the industry is lights-out manufacturing, meaning that the plants have the ability to run autonomously.

Lear's returns to shareholders, including repurchases of shares, have been very impressive. Lear is one of the few companies where I believe its buyback strategy is very good - because it focuses it on years or times when the stock is objectively cheap from a historical (and other metrics) perspective.

Most articles for Lear as of late have been positive - but many of the recent ones in 2025, have also been written by me. The upside that I see is partially based on what the company sees it can achieve, and partially a reversal to a fair valuation (which has now, to some extent, happened). The company's targets include an expansion of leadership in its seating operations, coupled with increases in EBIT through focusing of the company portfolio.

The perhaps core risk which I am not a big fan of, is some of the company's growth base. Much of both the expected growth, and the recent growth, has or will come from Chinese OEM's.

This means that Lear is meaningfully correlated (more so than last year), to Chinese OEM's, and that if there's a downturn in Chinese automotive, the growth that's being forecasted is unlikely to materialize as expected or as forecasted by the company.

And anyone arguing that this risk is not significant is, in my view, missing some key parts about Chinese BEV brands. The risks which are evident include significant overcapacity, margin problems due to increasing cuts in pricing, the potential for the Chinese government to reduce automotive market subsidies, which is already happening as we speak, as well as what I believe to be a fundamental lack of likelihood of international expansion (backed by politics, which are pushing against potential expansion) to any degree that would justify a higher valuation.

The reason why I'm not valuing Lear much, much higher, is because I am bearish on Chinese automotive. If you're bullish, then you probably would have a more positive view here, where the company's growth targets of double digits may be met. But because this goes against the data coming out of China, I consider this to be risky.

Lear is still attractive, despite these risks. Recent results mostly confirm this. Top-line results, meaning sales, are up for the last period, and despite a small drop in EPS (Adjusted), company cash flow was up by more than 100% YoY. Both segments saw positive momentum, and the cash flow was the third-ever highest quarter OCF, causing an increase in 2025E FCF. Appeal for its segments and products was confirmed with new business awards - $1.1B of it in the smaller E-systems segment alone.

It also continues to receive awards for seating and system innovations, and it achieves finishes and results that not even Adient can compete with - such as the J.D Power 2025 seat quality/satisfaction study, which it won for the third consecutive year in 2025.

On these bases, I consider this company to be an attractive business to invest in even now, as long as we're getting the company at the right or a cheap price.

What's a cheap price

So, a cheap price, going by my last target of $150/share, is still a long way to go from here. Despite significant returns, and a recovery from double-digit share price levels, I believe Lear at 9.66x P/E to be meaningfully undervalued.

The 5-year average is 14-15x P/E, implying an upside to a fair value target of no less than $240/share, justifying my $150 PT. Even using the longer-term 20-year average P/E as a forecast basis, that puts us at over $190/share, which also justifies my PT at a 19% upside.

And that, dear readers, is the lowest possible target estimate I would consider valid here.

Compare this to the upside that a company like Adient has, with a P/E that depends on 20-30x P/E premiumization on a forward basis. Here we don't depend on that sort of premiumization, and we also get a yield. I believe the only companies in this industry more attractive than Lear, are companies with a significantly different risk/reward ratio, by which I mean higher risk. As an alternative, I could name Valeo (VEOEY), which I technically consider to be more attractive, and with a better yield, but at what risk?

Valeo may have a better upside, but its risk is not appealing given its end markets as well as size. Unlike Lear, where technologies and products are mostly technology-agnostic (meaning they are needed regardless how your vehicle is powered or used), Valeo has more dependencies and also significant other differences, not the least of which is a BB rating instead of investment-grade.

That's why I own Valeo, but at a much smaller stake - and I'm fully cognizant of the risks involved.

My point, dear reader, is know your risk, know your exposure, know your expectations.

Don't invest in something because A says so, and you don't know what to expect. Lear, in my view, is likely to return market-beating rates of return over the medium (5-year) period, while paying an attractive yield, and while not offering, in my view, too much volatility. Unlike some peers like Adient, it does not depend on premiumization; it's a proven business with decades of appeal, and the current trends speak in favor of its valuation.

The risks are there, but the valuation is attractive despite this. Even in a scenario of very little growth, which could happen due to unattractive China trends, you're unlikely to see capital losses - just less profit.

Lear has no debt maturities until 2 years from now, it has low cost of cash at below 4% for its bonds, and has over $3B in 2025A liquidity. Also, it's on track for very solid conversion rates, and it continues to pay dividends. It's a healthy, solid, well-managed company.

The risks to this solid company are mostly external, and are as follows.

Risks to My Thesis

The primary risks to my thesis are related to recovery, or the lack of it, in LVP/automotives. Lear's production cycles are tied to cyclical trends, and when LVP production or demand goes down, as we're seeing now (still, yes, in 2026), downturns are entirely possible. The core risk to Lear here is, as I would characterize it, aside from global LVP, China exposure.

The company has gone on record increasing its China exposure step by step, with a target of almost half by 2027. I believe this to be a mistake on Lear's part, given the automotive specifics of the Chinese market, and the risks coming here. Secondary risks to consider are input costs, namely base metals and components, but these are really secondary.

Beyond these twin risks, I don't see much that could impact Lear in the longer term, and as long as we have automotives, I believe that Lear is not in a place where anything could derail it fundamentally.

My PT of $150/share remains - all of the upside, and all of the risk is included previously, because not much of this is new. What I am doing is a re-iteration following significant outperformance to the market.

Thesis

Lear is a fundamentally attractive business with an investment-grade credit rating, good yield, and conservative upside. Given that the company has spent the last few years moving from a valuation of over 15x P/E to a valuation that still, as of early 2026, is still sub-10x P/E normalized, I still view this company as meaningfully undervalued despite a market-beating TSR of over 30% in less than 6 months.

Because of this, I am maintaining my target for Lear at $150/share. I believe this price target takes into consideration China risks as well as other LVP-related risks we're seeing, including what may come from FX. Because the USD is so attractive to invest in now, with a lower pairing than in over 3 years, I'm looking at buying more of the business.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. Based on this and a price target of $150/share, where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me, I give the company a "Buy" rating as of January 2026.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Tracking Baillie Gifford's 13F Portfolio - Q4 2025 Update
data.description:
This article is part of a series that provides an ongoing analysis of the changes made to Baillie Gifford’s 13F portfolio on a quarterly basis. It is based on their regulatory 13F Form filed on 01/23/2026. Please visit our Tracking Baillie Gifford 13F Portfolio series to get an idea of their investment philosophy and our last update for the fund’s moves during Q3 2025. The 13F securities represent roughly 40% of their overall Assets Under Management. The portfolio is diversified with around 300 13F securities. 45 of those positions are significantly large (more than ~0.5% of the portfolio) and they are the focus of this article.

This quarter, Baillie Gifford’s 13F portfolio value decreased from ~$135B to ~$120B. The top three holdings are at ~18% while the top five are close to ~27% of the 13F assets: NVIDIA, MercadoLibre, Amazon, Spotify, and Sea Limited.

Note: Although the following positions are really small compared to the overall size of the portfolio, it is significant that they have sizable ownership stakes: American Superconductor (AMSC), Appian Corporation (APPN), Aurora Innovation (AUR), Copa Holdings (COPA), Coursera (COUR), Denali Therapeutics (DNLA), Dutch Bros (BROS), Gingko Bioworks (DNA), Lemonade (LMND), MakeMyTrip (MMYT), Mobileye (MBLY), Oddity Tech (ODD), Recursion Pharma (RXRX), Remitly Global (RELY), Sana Biotech (SANA), Sweetgreen (SG), Tencent Music Entertainment (TME), Upwork (UPWK), and Wayfair Inc. (W).

Stake Increases:

MercadoLibre Inc. (MELI): MELI position goes back to 2010 when a 2.25M share stake was built at prices between ~$35 and ~$73. The position size peaked at 6.56M shares in 2014. The stake increase happened at prices between ~$60 and ~$140. The two years through Q2 2023 saw a ~39% increase at prices between ~$635 and ~$1946. There was a ~11% selling during Q4 2023. Q1 2025 also saw a similar reduction at prices between ~$1740 and ~$2260. It is their second largest 13F position at 5.83% of the portfolio. The stock currently trades at ~$2137. There was a ~12% trimming over the last two quarters while this quarter saw a ~5% stake increase.

Note: they have a ~7% ownership stake in the business.

NU Holdings (NU): The 3.58% NU position saw a ~48% increase during Q1 2024 at prices between $8.09 and $12.25. That was followed by a ~10% increase in the next quarter at prices between $10.48 and $12.92. Q3 2024 also saw a ~13% stake increase at prices between $10.71 and $15.14. The next quarter saw a ~7% further increase at prices between $10.24 and $15.89. Q1 2025 also saw a ~18% stake increase at prices between ~$10 and ~$14. The stock is now at ~$18. There was minor trimming in the last two quarters while in this quarter there was a ~2% stake increase.

Netflix Inc. (NFLX): NFLX is a 2.88% of the portfolio stake established in the 2015-2016 timeframe at prices up to ~$13. There was a ~20% trimming over the two years through Q1 2023. That was followed by a ~15% reduction in the next quarter at prices between ~$32 and ~$45. The three quarters through Q1 2024 saw a ~20% further selling. The last five quarters also saw further reduction while in this quarter there was a minor increase. The stock currently trades at ~$86.

Note: The prices quoted are adjusted for the 10-for-1 stock split in November.

Duolingo (DUOL): The 0.67% of the portfolio position saw a ~29% stake increase during the last quarter at prices between ~$257 and ~$468. That was followed by a ~72% further increase this quarter at prices between ~$166 and ~$353. The stock currently trades below the bottom of those ranges at ~$156. They are building this position against falling prices.

Alphabet Inc. (GOOG), Atlassian Corp (TEAM), Axon Enterprise (AXON), CoStar Group (CSGP), Rocket Lab (RKLB), Samsara (IOT), and The Ensign Group (ENSG): These positions were increased during the quarter.

Stake Decreases:

NVIDIA Inc. (NVDA): A large stake in NVDA was first purchased in 2016 at prices up to ~$28. The position has seen periodic selling since. Recent activity follows. Q2 2024 saw a ~20% reduction at prices between ~$76 and ~$136. That was followed by a ~23% reduction during the next quarter at prices between ~$99 and ~$135. Q4 2024 also saw a ~17% selling at prices between ~$117 and ~$149. The next quarter saw another ~11% reduction at prices between ~$107 and ~$149. The stock currently trades at ~$188 and the stake is the largest 13F position at 6.80% of the portfolio. The last two quarters saw minor trimming.

Amazon.com Inc. (AMZN): AMZN stake was a very small position purchased in 2004. The period through 2009 saw a large position built at very low prices. Since 2014, the position has seen selling. Recent activity follows. The last three years saw minor trimming most quarters. The stock is now at ~$239, and the stake is at 5.64% of the portfolio.

Shopify Inc. (SHOP): The 4.17% of the portfolio SHOP stake was built in the 2017-2019 timeframe at very low prices. Since then, the position had seen minor trimming. Recent activity follows. There was a ~10% selling during Q2 2024 while in the next quarter there was a ~15% stake increase at prices between ~$52 and ~$81. Q2 2025 saw a roughly one-third reduction at prices between ~$70 and ~$117. There was a ~6% trimming during the last quarter. This quarter also saw a ~12% selling at prices between ~$136 and ~$182. The stock currently trades at ~$138. They are harvesting gains.

SEA Ltd. (SE): The 4.11% position in SE was increased by 20% during Q3 2023 at prices between ~$35 and ~$67. The next quarter saw another 31% increase at prices between ~$35 and ~$47. Q3 2024 saw a further ~15% stake increase at prices between ~$60 and ~$95. The stock is now at ~$125. The last few quarters have seen minor trimming.

Note: they have a ~7% ownership stake in SEA Ltd.

Spotify Inc. (SPOT): The original SPOT position goes back to a Series G funding round in 2015 when the company was valued at ~$8B. SPOT had an IPO in 2018. Shares started trading at ~$150 and currently go for ~$513. The position has wavered. Recent activity follows. There was a ~25% reduction during Q2 2024 at prices between ~$266 and ~$327. That was followed by a ~16% selling during Q4 2024 at prices between ~$363 and ~$502. The next quarter also saw a ~18% reduction at prices between ~$458 and ~$648. There was a ~11% further trimming during Q2 2025 at prices between ~$475 and ~$785. The last quarter also saw a similar reduction at prices between ~$616 and ~$770. There was a minor ~4% trimming this quarter. They are harvesting gains.

AppLovin (APP): The 3.45% APP stake was primarily built during Q4 2024 at prices between ~$131 and ~$402. The next quarter saw a ~30% stake increase at prices between ~$238 and ~$510. That was followed by a ~20% increase during Q2 2025 at prices between ~$201 and ~$429. The stock is now at ~$524. There was minor trimming in the last two quarters.

PDD Holdings Inc. (PDD): PDD is a 2.92% of the portfolio stake built over the three years through 2021 at prices between ~$19 and ~$197. There was a ~11% stake increase during Q2 2023 and that was followed by a ~15% increase in the next quarter at prices between ~$68 and ~$103. Q4 2023 saw a ~6% increase while in the next quarter there was a similar reduction. There was a ~14% reduction during Q2 2025 at prices between ~$88 and ~$122. The last quarter also saw a ~7% trimming at prices between ~$102 and ~$135. The stock currently trades at ~$106. This quarter saw a minor ~2% trimming.

Coupang Inc. (CPNG): CPNG had an IPO in Q1 2021. Shares started trading at ~$48 and currently go for ~$20. The 2.86% of the portfolio stake was built over the five quarters through Q1 2022 at prices between ~$17.50 and ~$48.50. The position was increased by 22% during Q4 2023 at prices between $15.17 and $18.11. That was followed by a ~16% increase in the next quarter at prices between $13.84 and $19.62. The last few quarters saw minor trimming.

Cloudflare Inc. (NET): The 2.76% NET stake was purchased during Q2 and Q3 2020 at prices between ~$21 and ~$42. The three quarters through Q2 2022 saw a ~19% selling while the next two quarters saw a ~10% increase. There was a ~23% stake increase during Q2 2023 at prices between ~$41 and ~$71. That was followed by a ~9% increase during Q2 2024 at prices between $67.43 and $97. There was a ~14% reduction during Q1 2025 at prices between ~$111 and ~$177. The last quarter saw similar selling at prices between ~$179 and ~$230. There was a ~23% further reduction this quarter at prices between ~$181 and ~$260. The stock currently trades at ~$173.

Note: they have a ~5% ownership stake in the business.

Meta Platforms (META): The 2.41% stake in META was increased by 12% during Q3 2023 at prices between ~$283 and ~$325. That was followed by a ~25% increase during the next quarter at prices between ~$288 and ~$358. Q1 2024 also saw a ~14% increase at prices between ~$344 and ~$512. There was a ~17% reduction this quarter at prices between ~$581 and ~$759. The stock currently trades at ~$659.

Microsoft Corp (MSFT): MSFT is now a 2.23% of the portfolio stake. The position saw a large ~45% stake increase during Q3 2024 at prices between ~$394 and ~$467. There was a ~14% selling during Q2 2025 at prices between ~$345 and ~$501. The stock currently trades at ~$466. The last two quarters have seen minor trimming.

Ferrari NV (RACE): RACE was a ~18M shares stake that was established in 2016 at prices between $34 and $58. The period through 2020 saw the stake reduced to ~13.5M shares at higher prices. There was a ~15% selling during Q2 2021 at prices between ~$199 and ~$217. The three quarters through Q1 2023 saw another ~30% reduction at prices between ~$180 and ~$270. The stock currently trades at ~$339. There was further selling in the last eleven quarters. They are harvesting gains.

Alnylam Pharma (ALNY): The bulk of the 0.86% of the portfolio stake in ALNY was built in 2019 at prices between ~$69 and ~$124. There was a ~20% selling during Q3 2024 at prices between ~$234 and ~$287. That was followed by a ~17% reduction during the next quarter at prices between ~$232 and ~$301. Q1 2025 also saw a ~10% selling at prices between ~$233 and ~$291. There was a ~17% reduction during the next quarter at prices between ~$206 and ~$327. The stock currently trades at ~$358. The last two quarters also saw further trimming. They are harvesting gains.

data.title: Ascendis: Why I'm Sceptical On Takeover Rumours, Long-Term Targets
data.description:
Investment Overview

The stock of Ascendis Pharma (ASND) has risen in value by >1,000% over the past decade, climbing from a price of ~$20, to >$235 per share at the time of writing. Stock is also up >85% on a 1-year basis.

I first covered Ascendis in a note for Seeking Alpha in April 2023, awarding stock a Buy rating with stock trading at ~$72 per share, having collapsed in value after a delay to the FDA's regulatory review for commercial approval of TransCon PTH (palopegteriparatide) in hypoparathyroidism.

As a reminder, re its Q3 2025 quarterly report / 10Q filing:

Our TransCon technologies are designed to combine the benefits of conventional prodrug and sustained release technologies to solve the fundamental limitations seen in other approaches to extending duration of a drug’s action in the body, with the goal of developing highly differentiated product candidates based on efficacy, safety, tolerability, and convenience.

In addition to retaining the original mode of action of the parent drug and potentially supporting dosing frequency from daily up to six months or more, we believe that predictable release over time can improve treatment safety and efficacy, increase the likelihood of clinical development success, and provide intellectual property benefits.

By the time of my next note, Ascendis stock traded at $193 per share, justifying my Buy call. Ultimately, after a delay, TransCon PTH was approved in August 2024, and is also approved in Europe. It is the second product Ascendis has commercialised, and is marketed and sold as Yorvipath. Skytrofa (TransCon hGH) has also been approved to treat growth hormone deficiency, since 2021.

Announcing its Q3 earnings and business updates last November 12th, Ascendis revealed revenues of €143.1 million for YORVIPATHand €50.7 million for SKYTROFA. On a nine-month basis, the company reported $443.5m of total commercial product revenue, total revenues of $472.6m, a net loss of €61.0 million, or €1 per share, and a cash position of €539 million.

Additionally, management updated on its pipeline as follows:

TransCon® CNP (navepegritide) under FDA Priority Review for the treatment of children with achondroplasia with PDUFA date of November 30, 2025

This date has in fact been pushed back to the end of February this year, due to an FDA request for a revised protocol for the post-marketing study.

At the JP Morgan (JPM) Healthcare Conference earlier this month, Ascendis revealed in its presentation that product revenues earned in 2025 were ~€683 million, with Yorvipath earning ~€477 million, and Skytrofa ~€206 million, and that total revenue amounted to ~€720 million. Gross margin was reportedly 87%, and full-year operating expenses were estimated to be ~€762 million. Cash position was reported as ~€616 million.

During his speech at the conference, Ascendis' CEO Jan Mikkelsen told the audience:

In December last year we really had a celebration. Ascendis Pharma turned out to be 18 years. At least in Denmark, it means one thing, you throw your children away and they are financially independent. That is the key thing. You don't fund them more. That was exactly what happened at that time for Ascendis.

After 18 years. We came to the states, building on our TransCon technology, build it on our R&D engine, building on our late stage clinical development, building on what we call the last states of the rocket, how really to do global commercialization. We came to the stage where we had 2 quarters of profitable business.

In summary, Ascendis has been able to build a company with two approved products, and a third potentially to be approved this year, is generating ~$850m dollars of revenue per annum, and looks set to become net profitable, perhaps this year.

The business has risen to a market cap valuation of $14.4bn at the time of writing, and rumours surfaced towards the end of this week that it may have become a takeover target for a Swiss based Pharma company. We may speculate that e.g. Roche (OTCQX:RHHBY) and Novartis (NVS), the Swiss Pharma giants, may be one of the interested parties, although nothing has been confirmed.

In the remainder of this note I'll take a closer look at Ascendis' business and speculate on whether a Pharma may be tempted to buy out Ascendis, and what price it may be prepared to pay if so, and what that may mean for the share price trajectory of the company.

Ascendis - Strong Technology, Strong Platform

Ascendis has certainly rewarded long-term shareholders' faith in its technology and products, and it is now focused on its "Vision 2030" goals, as shown in the slide below:

As we can see, the long-term revenue goal is ~€5bn of revenues, and as we can see below, multiple studies of its three drugs are ongoing, which, if successful, ought to lead to more approvals and label expansions, for example treating children, adolescents and infants, and across different territories also.

By 2035, Ascendis plans to have eight or more approved products, for example an IL-2 inhibitor and FGF-23 degrader (according to its presentation), and has established a partnership with Novo Nordisk (NVO), marketer and seller of the multi-billion dollar per annum selling weight loss / Type 2 Diabetes drugs Wegovy / Ozempic, to develop obesity / metabolic candidates, and a subsidiary company, Eyconis, to develop opthalmology candidates.

Although the company's success in bringing two products to market, and likely, a third in 2026, and achieving close to $1bn of revenues is impressive, when we consider that Ascendis' price to sales ratio is ~16.5x, and the company is not yet profitable, a >$14bn valuation of the company does feel high.

Moving from ~$850m of revenues to >$5.9bn in only a few years is a tough task - in fact, I cannot think of a pharma company (outside of the "Big Pharma" giants) that has achieved such growth in the past decade other than Moderna (MRNA), thanks to its development of a COVID vaccine.

Therefore I will preach some caution here, although if the revenue figure is met, Ascendis' valuation ought to surge past $20bn and possibly even $30bn as a result, and therefore any company paying ~$15bn for the company today would be getting a bargain.

Speaking at the JPM conference re the market opportunity in parathyroidism, the CEO stated:

In U.S., 70,000 to 90,000 (patients), which are what we call the lowest number. Some people actually believe this is 100% higher. Europe Direct 150,000 to 200,000 and international market, a huge market. And this is where we're building up a global commercialization.

So currently, we see the big driver of the revenue increase is mainly been from the U.S. side. But what we see by long-term basic that ex U.S. will take over more and more because it's much more fragmented, it takes a longer time to penetrate, but you will really see the high value because of the large patient population.

In Q1 2025, Yorvipath revenues were just €45m, so we can see that revenues are rising rapidly and we might reason that further approvals and label expansions are likely, based on a well established strong efficacy and safety record, both clinically and commercially.

Therefore as discussed, there is a lot that is derisked with Ascendis. With that said, if for any reason TransCon CNP is rejected for approval at the end of next month, I would expect a sharp market sell-off, and doubts cast over the underlying technology, which to date has been viewed very positively.

According to Ascenis, however, the pivotal study of TransCon CNP:

demonstrated significant improvements in linear growth and body proportionality, as well as benefits beyond linear growth compared to placebo

The therapy was generally well tolerated, with generally mild TEAEs and "exceeds historical benchmarks for growth without compromising safety or tolerability at 52 weeks", Ascendis says.

As we can see below, there are multiple intriguing data and approval catalysts arriving in 2026, although my feeling is that the upcoming PDUFA date in February may be the most important in terms of Ascendis' share price trajectory in 2026.

Concluding Thoughts - Am I Upgrading My Rating On Ascendis In 2026?

In my view, Ascendis is a good example of a company that is either worth significantly more, or significantly less than its current market cap valuation of ~$14.4bn.

For example, an unprofitable company trading at a P/S ratio of >15x feels overvalued, in my view, at least based on what it is achieving in the present, and in terms of what may be achieved in the future, we have to apply a discount to account for the risks that management does not meet its ambitious forecasts, that approvals are not secured, that another biotech does not launch a competing product, etc.

On the other hand, if management succeeds in driving a >40% annual revenue growth rate between 2026 - 2030, it will achieve its targets, and market dynamics, particularly with regard to hypoparathyroidism, certainly suggest the market is underpenetrated. Analysts have speculated TransCon CNP could be a "blockbuster" (>$1bn revenues per annum) also. As discussed, for a $5bn revenue per annum company, a valuation of $14.5bn feels too low.

Nevertheless, I am sticking to my Hold recommendation as I don't feel there is quite enough concrete evidence to justify the ambitious revenue targets, and my estimate of what revenue the company may generate by 2030 falls short of management's estimates, simply because so few drugs drive long-term CAGR revenue of >40%.

Rumours of a takeover - also in my experience - fail to result in a confirmed takeover more often than not, and therefore, I think the best strategy is to wait at least until the February PDUFA date for TransCon CNP (I believe the market has priced in a successful outcome), and if rumours still persist at that time, re-evaluate the situation.

At this time, however, I can make more of a case for shares retreating in value - readers should also pay close attention to any revenue / income guidance also, which I suspect may slightly underwhelm the market given the stated long-term targets - than I can for further gains being realised, at this time, so my best call is Hold - by the end of 2026, the overall picture around whether the Vision 2030 goals are achievable ought to be substantially clearer.

data.title: HFRO: Resilient Real Estate Fund But Struggling In High Interest Rate Environment
data.description:
Overview

When I previously covered Highland Opportunities and Income Fund (HFRO), I issued a hold rating due to the challenging macroenvironment. Since my last coverage, the Fed has initiated interest rate cuts, and this has created the expectation for lower interest rates over the next twelve months. Therefore, I wanted to revisit the fund's overall value proposition and ability to navigate challenging conditions going forward. The fund primarily focuses on real estate securities, which has been a sector that continues to lag behind the rest of the market.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that HFRO's share price has increased by nearly 9.6%. The fund has been able to hold onto some of the positive price growth experienced around mid-2025. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 19.6% over the same time frame. HFRO now offers investors a starting dividend yield of 7.5% and issues payouts on a monthly basis. Based on the latest financials, the fund's dividend payouts are likely to remain stable despite variable coverage.

The fund continues to trade at a discounted price to NAV valuation, which can be an indication that it's a solid time to accumulate shares. However, a position in HFRO makes the most sense for investors that believe interest rates will continue to decline over the next twelve months. In a scenario where interest rates do not decline, the fund's risk profile can increase. For instance, HFRO's dividends may get cut if the underlying earnings do not improve over the next year. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that HFRO implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fact sheet, HFRO has total managed assets of $880M that are allocated across a blended portfolio of assets. The fund's primary objective is to grow capital while providing a high current income. In order to achieve this goal, the fund chooses to focus on real estate securities and different forms of debt securities. HFRO allocates at least 25% of its assets to securities that are directly secured by physical real estate. The portfolio's breakdown by investment type is as follows:

  • Real Estate: 65.7%
  • Equity: 22.1%
  • Loan: 11.6%
  • Collateralized Loan Obligations: 0.7%

What stands out to me is that HFRO also has some direct equity exposure. This equity exposure aligns the fund to see capital appreciation during favorable market cycles for real estate. Expanding on this, we can see that HFRO's real estate is also spread across different operating segments, such as multifamily, life sciences, and storage properties. Although the real estate investments span across different operating areas, real estate as a whole generally shares the same vulnerability to higher interest rates. The sector is arguably one of the most dependent on access to cheap debt financing, and the current environment of elevated rates could be suppressing performance.

When looking at the top positions within the fund, we can see that HFRO provides a clear breakdown of its positions and how much of the holding is positioned as equity or debt. The portfolio is primarily allocated to a pool of private entities, which may be attractive for investors looking to get access to securities that aren't commonly available. These private entities also have the potential for growth, but they lack the same transparency that public holdings would provide.

Valuation & Performance Insights

At the time of my last coverage, HFRO traded at a large discount to NAV of 42.21%. Despite the share price increase over the last few months, the fund now trades at a slightly larger discount to NAV of 47%. This larger discount can be an indication of an underlying price disconnect and can be an opportunity for long-term investors to accumulate on the weakness. Referring to the red line on the graph below, we can see that HFRO currently trades well below the average price-to-NAV level of peers within the same industry.

In order to get a better understanding of why the large discount to NAV pricing exists, I thought it would be helpful to review the latest earnings. While these earnings may be a bit dated, they reveal some clear vulnerabilities of the fund that must be addressed. For instance, investments at fair value continue to decline, while the total debt outstanding simultaneously increases. HFRO's net assets now sit around the lowest levels seen over the last few years, while the debt-to-equity ratio has more than doubled to 0.39x since Q3 of 2023.

With the additional debt taken on, HFRO has been able to increase its total investment income up to $11.8M. However, the actual net investment income amounts to $8.9M, which is a less impressive increase from the prior quarter's net investment income of $7.5M. This implies that the fund's operating costs are elevated and may be shrinking the internal spread.

As part of the fund's strategy, HFRO utilizes leverage in an effort to amplify its earnings. The use of leverage currently amounts to 27.81% of assets, which is equivalent to ~$245M. During this period of elevated interest rates, I would consider this to be an aggressive use of leverage since the fund's operating spread between the cost of its debt and the yield collected from securities may be thinner. Additionally, leverage can always amplify losses if allocated to securities that don't perform as anticipated. This use of leverage has ultimately amplified the fund's sensitivity to interest rates, as we can see below. The idea is that future interest rate cuts will lead to optimism around real estate, as well as improve operating spreads, which can help HFRO's share price regain some positive momentum.

Lastly, the fund's priority on distributions does not actually lead to higher returns over time. The combination of the use of leverage, sensitivity to interest rates, and inconsistent earnings means that the fund can find itself in a position where it is paying out more than it actually earns. The end result is a share price that can decline more rapidly than a more traditional sector-tracking ETF, such as the Real Estate Select Sector SPDR® Fund ETF (XLRE). Additionally, the focus on income-producing securities means that HFRO's ability to capture price growth is fairly limited compared to a fund like XLRE.

As we can see below, HFRO's total return since inception sits at a loss of 21.3%, even when including all distributions paid. The share price erosion has been severe since inception, with the share price being down more than 60% over the same time frame. The fund severely underperforms XLRE over the same time frame, so the focus on income may translate to underperformance if the health of the real estate sector doesn't improve.

Dividend is Too Generous

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.0385 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 7.5%. Looking at the dividend history below, we can see that distributions were severely reduced at the start of 2024, which is likely due to the poor earnings of the fund. If we take this monthly distribution and multiply it by three months, we get a quarterly payout of $0.1155 per share. Referring back to the same earnings report, HFRO was able to generate net investment income per share of $0.16. This indicates that earnings currently support the distribution at a coverage rate of 139%. Therefore, I don't think the dividend is under the threat of a reduction in the near term.

Despite the large distribution cut in the past, I believe that payouts may still be a bit too generous. This is because the fund is essentially paying out most of its earnings, and this limits the ability to use free capital towards new investments. Additionally, the fund's underlying NAV continues its downward trend over the last year. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • Q3 2024: $12.96 per share
  • Q4 2024: $12.00 per share
  • Q1 2025: $12.35 per share
  • Q2 2025: $11.38 per share
  • Q3 2025: $11.48 per share

Lastly, investors should consider the fact that distributions may not offer tax efficiency. Since the distributions are being supported by real estate securities and interest income, the distributions are likely classified as ordinary dividends, which have the highest tax burden for investors. Unfortunately, there is not an updated section 19(a) notice that is available at this time to confirm this.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that HFRO remains a hold at this time. The fund trades at a massive discount to NAV valuation due to the challenging interest rate environment. The latest earnings report indicates that net investment income still supports the distributions, but there is certainly room for improvement. For instance, the fund's total outstanding debt has increased substantially over the last few years, and total net assets have declined. Since interest rates remain elevated, the fund's operating spread may be thin due to the inclusion of leverage and floating rate exposure. However, future interest rate cuts can potentially serve as a catalyst for growth in the future. A lower cost of debt may improve the fund's operating spread and increase optimism of the real estate sector.

Portfolio Positioning

Portfolio anchored on quality amid elevated market valuations

We continue to maintain a balanced, quality-anchored positioning in the portfolio. Our bottom-up fundamental assessment drives this risk-aware exposure across industries that we believe will benefit from long-term thematic tailwinds. We also maintain ample exposure to defensive holdings that will preserve capital should markets experience increased volatility or a pullback in the coming quarters.

The Fund's largest sector overweights are in Financials, Health Care and Information Technology. Our three largest underweights remain in Energy (no exposure), Consumer Staples and Consumer Discretionary.

Within Financials, our overall portfolio weight and stock level exposures remain largely unchanged, with majority exposure in companies that do not take balance sheet risk and are likely to be less affected by an economic slowdown.

During the quarter, we modestly increased our overweight in the Health Care sector primarily driven by the attractive risk/reward opportunity, especially as the sector emerges from several headwinds faced over the past three years.

Our overweight in Information Technology was modestly lower versus the third quarter as we continue to realize gains in select long-term secular winners. While we remain constructive on the sector’s structural growth drivers, we are also cognizant of valuation levels, particularly as market gains have broadened across a wider array of beneficiaries in recent quarters.

Across both Consumer Discretionary and Consumer Staples, our underweight positioning remained largely unchanged over the quarter. We believe our overweight in Financials, particularly in Payments, continues to provide an attractive risk-adjusted means of participating in consumer spending trends.

Additionally, we reduced our underweight positioning in Utilities and Industrials with the addition of CMS Energy and Waste Management. CMS’s investment plans are expected to accelerate earnings growth over the coming years, within a constructive regulatory environment. Waste Management’s wide moat, attractive valuation and commitment to environmental innovation offer a defensive business model with long-term upside potential.

Outlook

Market environment favors continued outperformance for value stocks

Value stocks outperformed growth stocks in the fourth quarter as the Russell 1000 Value index rose 3.81% compared to a 1.12% rise in the Russell 1000 Growth index. We expect the current market environment to continue favoring value stocks. High market valuations for growth stocks, a relatively benign economic backdrop, potential for lower rates and stimulative policy levers are all supportive of attractive risk/reward potential in value stocks.

Moreover, we believe the once-in-a-generation AI investments being made today will, over time, drive productivity gains, business model innovation and earnings growth across a much broader range of industries than is being appreciated by investors today. AI has the potential to impact every sector, not just technology, akin to earlier waves of automation and technology adoption. The timing of these benefits admittedly remains uncertain, which underpins the Fund's diversified exposure across sectors and across time—some will benefit this year, some over three years or longer. However, what we believe is more certain is that regardless of the sector, companies that have a strong track record of investing in innovation and the scale to justify these upfront investments will eventually emerge stronger through AI. This was true in prior innovation cycles, and this time will be no different.

The high-quality companies we own in the Value Equity Fund—across Banking, Insurance, Retail, Pharma, Medical Devices or Agricultural Equipment, among others—all possess the scale to invest ahead of peers and a demonstrated ability to translate investments into enduring shareholder value.

Our time-tested investment process with a disciplined focus on assessing both risk and return has served us well over the last two decades. As we enter the Fund’s 21st year, we remain confident that the portfolio is well positioned to navigate both macroeconomic and thematic crosscurrents. We will continue to focus on identifying and investing in quality businesses trading at attractive relative valuations in the pursuit of strong long-term returns.

2025 Review

2025 will be a year to forget for the Dividend Kings as, on average, they only mustered a 4.91% total return. While earning a positive return by being invested in some of the most tenured dividend growers is never a bad thing, underperforming the broad equity market is seldom an easy thing to swallow. The SPDR® S&P 500® ETF (SPY) finished last year with a total return of 17.72%, far outpacing the average Dividend King.

Despite the lackluster performance last year, 14 Dividend Kings still managed to deliver superior total returns relative to SPY.

Best Dividend Kings in 2025

As of year-end 2025, nineteen Dividend Kings have achieved double-digit total returns, but only fourteen outperformed SPY. These were the best-performing Kings:

  1. Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) +47.49%
  2. Nucor Corporation (NUE) +42.05%
  3. Parker-Hannifin (PH) +39.54%
  4. National Fuel Gas (NFG) +35.32%
  5. Canadian Utilities Limited (CDUAF) +35.10%
  6. AbbVie (ABBV) +33.13%
  7. Fortis (FTS) +29.62%
  8. The Gorman-Rupp Company (GRC) +28.26%
  9. Walmart (WMT) +24.49%
  10. Black Hills Corp. (BKH) +23.90%
  11. Northwest Natural Holding Company (NWN) +23.69%
  12. Archer-Daniels-Midland (ADM) +18.30%
  13. Altria Group (MO) +18.20%
  14. Tootsie Roll (TR) +17.87%

Of the remaining 42 Dividend Kings, 5 have a double-digit positive gain, 8 have single-digit gains, and 29 are in the red. The worst-performing Dividend King last year was Stepan Company (SCL) with a loss of 24.57%. Not too far from the bottom was Target (TGT) with a loss of 24.46%. In total, 16 Dividend Kings posted double-digit losses.

2026 has kicked off very well for the Dividend Kings, as they are, on average, up 4.66% through January 23rd, compared to a gain of 1.07% for SPY.

The five best-performing Dividend Kings in January are:

  1. Stepan Company +18.18%
  2. Archer Daniel Midland +17.43%
  3. Lowe's Companies (LOW) +14.75%
  4. MSA Safety (MSA) +13.27%
  5. Nordson (NDSN) +12.86%

Dividend Growth

The average dividend growth rate for all of the Dividend Kings in 2025 was 5.81%. Several increases have already been announced and documented for 2026. Additionally, some of the Kings will organically pay a higher dividend amount in calendar year 2026, relative to 2025, just due to the timing of their increases last year. In total, 45 out of the 57 Kings already locked in another year of dividend growth in 2026, most without yet announcing a dividend increase.

As of today, the average dividend growth rate for all of the Dividend Kings in 2026 stands at 2.07%.

With not much to cover in the current year thus far, perhaps I can recap the 6 best dividend growth rates from the prior year.

Earnings Projections

Since last month's update, analyst 3-5 year earnings growth projections have trended lower by -0.24%, on average for all the Dividend Kings.

11 Dividend Kings are currently projected to grow earnings by double digits over the next 3-5 years.

  1. AbbVie +16.26%
  2. Abbott Laboratories +10.07%

  9. Stepan Company +28.24%
  10. S&P Global (SPGI) +12.52%
  11. Stanley Black & Decker (SWK) +14.52%

New Dividend King

With the new year, we also have one new Dividend King joining this coveted dividend growth list; that King is Pentair (PNR). Pentair has already increased its dividend in 2026 from $0.25 per share to $0.27 per share, or by 8%. The stock currently appears to be potentially 28% overvalued based on Dividend Yield Theory and sports a 6.68% future return estimate.

Promising Kings in 2026

In last month's update, as of December 25th, I identified 22 Dividend Kings who appeared to be both potentially undervalued and had a strong long-term return estimate. My article was published on December 28th, and from December 29th to January 23rd, these 22 Kings had an average total return of +3.45%, compared to +3.62% for all 57 Dividend Kings and +0.20% for SPY. Slightly worse than all of the Dividend Kings and far better than SPY.

While this short-term performance is interesting to review, these Kings are chosen with a long-term mindset as part of the expected future return is based on reversion back to the mean.
